title: Strange behavior with mountopt="metacopy=on" when kernel doesn't support metacopy
body:
### Issue Description

When `mountopt = "metacopy=on"` is configured in storage.conf and the kernel doesn't support metacopy, buildah reports that it will not use the native overlay implementation (as seen in `buildah info` output). But everything still works fine **even when fuse-overlayfs is not installed**.

My questions are:
1. Is buildah's report accurate? Does metacopy=on prevent it from using the native overlay?
2. If so, what is the fallback? It can't be fuse-overlayfs, as that is not installed in the environment

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Build the following Containerfile and tag it as `buildah-test:latest`. Notice that `fuse-overlayfs` is intentionally omitted

```Dockerfile
FROM registry.access.redhat.com/ubi10/go-toolset:1.25.3 AS go-build

USER 0

# Install dependencies for compiling buildah
RUN dnf -y install \
        bzip2 \
        glib2-devel \
        gpgme-devel \
        libassuan-devel \
        libseccomp-devel

WORKDIR /build

RUN git clone --branch=v1.42.2 https://github.com/containers/buildah && \
    cd buildah && \
    make bin/buildah


FROM registry.access.redhat.com/ubi10/ubi-minimal:10.1

RUN microdnf -y install \
        containers-common-extra \
        gpgme \
        libassuan \
        libseccomp

# RUN microdnf -y install fuse-overlayfs

COPY --from=go-build /build/buildah/bin/buildah /usr/local/bin/buildah

COPY <<EOF /etc/containers/storage.conf
[storage]
driver = "overlay"

[storage.options.overlay]
mountopt = "nodev,metacopy=on"
EOF

# Unset 'mountopt=nodev,metacopy=on', because it seems to prevent buildah from using native overlay
# RUN sed 's/^mountopt/#mountopt/' -i /etc/containers/storage.conf

ENV BUILDAH_ISOLATION=chroot
```

2. Run `buildah info` in the container

```bash
podman run --rm -ti -v /var/lib/containers/storage buildah-test:latest buildah info --log-level=debug
```

Relevant parts of output:

```text
DEBU[0000] [graphdriver] trying provided driver "overlay" 
DEBU[0000] overlay: test mount with multiple lowers succeeded 
DEBU[0000] Cached value indicated that overlay is supported 
INFO[0000] overlay: metacopy option not supported on this kernel, checked using options "nodev,metacopy=on" 
DEBU[0000] overlay: test mount indicated that metacopy is not being used 
INFO[0000] Not using native diff for overlay, this may cause degraded performance for building images: failed to mount overlay: invalid argument 
DEBU[0000] backingFs=btrfs, projectQuotaSupported=false, useNativeDiff=false, usingMetacopy=false 
DEBU[0000] Normalized platform linux/amd64 to {amd64 linux  [] } 
DEBU[0000] overlay: test mount indicated that volatile is being used 
DEBU[0000] Check for idmapped mounts support create mapped mount: mount_setattr /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay/compat3264015436/lower: operation not permitted
```

```json
        "GraphStatus": {
            "Backing Filesystem": "btrfs",
            "Native Overlay Diff": "false",
            "Supports d_type": "true",
            "Supports shifting": "false",
            "Supports volatile": "true",
            "Using metacopy": "false"
        },
```

3. Uncomment the `sed` command in the Containerfile, build the image again, run the same command again.

Now the output looks like this:

```txt
DEBU[0000] [graphdriver] trying provided driver "overlay" 
DEBU[0000] overlay: test mount with multiple lowers succeeded 
DEBU[0000] Cached value indicated that overlay is supported 
DEBU[0000] overlay: test mount indicated that metacopy is not being used 
DEBU[0000] backingFs=btrfs, projectQuotaSupported=false, useNativeDiff=true, usingMetacopy=false 
DEBU[0000] Normalized platform linux/amd64 to {amd64 linux  [] } 
DEBU[0000] overlay: test mount indicated that volatile is being used 
DEBU[0000] Check for idmapped mounts support create mapped mount: mount_setattr /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay/compat2594946589/lower: operation not permitted
```

```json
        "GraphStatus": {
            "Backing Filesystem": "btrfs",
            "Native Overlay Diff": "true",
            "Supports d_type": "true",
            "Supports shifting": "false",
            "Supports volatile": "true",
            "Using metacopy": "false"
        },
```

### Describe the results you received

With `metacopy=on`, buildah reports that it's not using native overlay, but still works even without `fuse-overlayfs`

### Describe the results you expected

Expected that buildah would either use native overlay or fail without `fuse-overlayfs`

### buildah version output

```yaml
Version:         1.42.2
Go Version:      go1.25.3 (Red Hat 1.25.3-1.el10_1)
Image Spec:      1.1.1
Runtime Spec:    1.2.1
CNI Spec:        1.1.0
libcni Version:  v1.3.0
image Version:   5.38.0
Git Commit:      c0cc97255c76f7a3d87bfdf5e7b97f013f0bfe85
Built:           Fri Jan 16 11:35:18 2026
OS/Arch:         linux/amd64
BuildPlatform:   linux/amd64
```

### buildah info output

```yaml
{
    "host": {
        "CgroupVersion": "v2",
        "Distribution": {
            "distribution": "\"rhel\"",
            "version": "10.1"
        },
        "MemFree": 2421260288,
        "MemTotal": 33056522240,
        "OCIRuntime": "crun",
        "SwapFree": 7105622016,
        "SwapTotal": 8589930496,
        "arch": "amd64",
        "cpus": 14,
        "hostname": "cd396c1e00fa",
        "kernel": "6.17.13-200.fc42.x86_64",
        "os": "linux",
        "rootless": true,
        "uptime": "75h 31m 10.26s (Approximately 3.12 days)",
        "variant": ""
    },
    "store": {
        "ContainerStore": {
            "number": 0
        },
        "GraphDriverName": "overlay",
        "GraphImageStore": "",
        "GraphOptions": [
            "overlay.mountopt=nodev,metacopy=on"
        ],
        "GraphRoot": "/var/lib/containers/storage",
        "GraphStatus": {
            "Backing Filesystem": "btrfs",
            "Native Overlay Diff": "false",
            "Supports d_type": "true",
            "Supports shifting": "false",
            "Supports volatile": "true",
            "Using metacopy": "false"
        },
        "GraphTransientStore": false,
        "ImageStore": {
            "number": 0
        },
        "RunRoot": "/run/containers/storage"
    }
}
```

### Provide your storage.conf

```toml
[storage]
driver = "overlay"

[storage.options.overlay]
mountopt = "nodev,metacopy=on"
```

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
The difference is in whether or not it'll use the native overlay diff (emphasis on that last word) implementation, which more or less tars up a layer's "upper" directory when attempting to construct a layer diff that represents the changes between that layer and the one it was based on, as opposed to "naive diff", which mounts them both and walks the directory trees looking for differences, which is expected to be slower.

Using fuse-overlayfs instead of the kernel's overlay implementation is a different thing, controlled by whether or not a "mount_program" option was used to force it, or if the built-in logic determined that the kernel's overlay implementation wasn't available at all.

I see, thanks for explaining!

Would it be right to assume that, in terms of performance: native overlay diff > native overlay with naive diff > fuse-overlayfs > vfs ?

I think that's a reasonable way to look at it - "native" diff is going to be faster, and the speed of "naive" diff is going to be affected by the filesystems under it.  I haven't done the numbers, but overlay and fuse-overlayfs having to do the behind-the-scenes work of constructing the contents of directories using data from multiple layers _might_ give vfs a slight edge there.
One more question - in my specific scenario, unsetting `metacopy=on` would likely improve performance, right? Since that seems to enable the native diff

I believe that it would make generating diffs faster.  The cost is that the benefit of the "metacopy" option (which speeds up changes to the filesystem that only change the metadata on an inode: permissions, timestamps, that sort of thing) will be lost.
Got it. That answers my questions, thanks!
